Workers at Honduran reception centers told researchers that if they had asked arriving deportees on any given day how many left children behind in the U.S., roughly 80 percent would raise their hands.

Once deported, reunification is not guaranteed by U.S. law or policy. Honduras lacks the infrastructure, funding, and information from the U.S. government needed to systematically reunify separated families.

Pregnant women in detention reported little to no prenatal care. One woman bled for several days inside a facility before being deported with no treatment. She arrived in Honduras in emergency condition requiring immediate hospitalization.

All four postpartum women researchers interviewed had been separated from their infants. One mother was separated from a baby less than two months old. None were given the opportunity to bring their children with them.

One mother filled out ICE Form 71-076 stating in writing that she wanted to bring her child with her. She was deported anyway, without her daughter, hands cuffed to her stomach through the night before her flight.

More than half of deported parents interviewed said ICE never asked if they had children at any point during their arrest, detention, or deportation. Some were told not to speak when they tried to volunteer that information.

ICE has its own policy (the Detained Parents Directive) requiring agents to ask anyone they arrest whether they have children and to give parents the opportunity to decide what happens to those children. Researchers found routine violations of both requirements.

The administration said it would target violent criminals. The data says otherwise. ICE confirms that 73% of detained individuals have no criminal convictions, and fewer than 5% have convictions for violent crimes.

Immigration detention has reached the highest level in U.S. history. Since January 2025, the number of people detained without any criminal record has increased by approximately 2,500 percent, according to ICE's own data.

Notable to the discourse, moderating on trans issues is not particularly high-leverage. 

The best things to move right on are (in order) racial preferences, teachers unions, sexual orientation curriculum in schools, letting in asylum-seekers, punishing non-violent criminals, and fossil fuels.

So the upshot here is that rather than moderating at random, Democrats should stick with conventional progressive defense of Social Security, Medicare, and Medicaid while also adopting views that are to the right of the entire existing caucus on a few key issues.

What is the single most effective issue to move to the center on? 

Affirmative action in college admissions — an issue that no Democrat in recent cycles has been moderate on. 

Obviously moderation doesn't work if you don't do it!

And to be clear "moderate" in this research does not mean "like Kamala Harris or Gavin Newsom as opposed to Bernie" — it means "be more right-wing than mainstream establishment Democrats." 

But it does also help explain why the observed benefits of moderation in recent cycles are small.

Sorry to give a less measured take, this research indicates that just adopting more moderate views at random — though definitely not optimal — will on average help you win elections. 

If you have no survey research or data or anything, just go with your gut and be more moderate.

As Morris has documented extensively, the electoral benefits of moderation, though real, are also not enormous. 

The paper suggests that moderating SELECTIVELY — on affirmative action, merit pay for teachers, punishing non-violent crime, and using fossil fuels — would be much more potent.

As @gelliottmorris.com says, the research offers more nuanced advice than "just be more moderate." 

It says that moderation is beneficial on average, but that the impact of moderation varies issue-to-issue and that on a minority of issues moderation actually backfires.

there’s a barely-concealed condescension that frames (white) mainstream discussion of Jesse Jackson - he was “complex”, “personal failings”, “self promotion” “ego” - that, functionally, denies him his rightful mantle: possibly the most important political figure of his era

over decades, Jackson: insisted on a multiracial coalition, poured material resources into enfranchising southern voters, helped figure out what civil rights looks like in northern urban contexts, and helped define the lane for black leaders within national D politics

the fruit of Jesse’s labor is apparent in so much of what comes after him: not just the rise of Obama, but also of Bernie and the current multi-racial progressive politics. He was the crucial bridge between 68 and our contemporary moment
